summary: Matched a large block of `src/melee/ft/ftcpuattack.c`, adding CPU attack/behavior routines and supporting type/header fixes. The decomp.dev bot reported 28 new `main/melee/ft/ftcpuattack` matches, +15536 matched bytes, and +0.40% matched code for GALE01. Supporting changes corrected several `ftCo_0A01` item helper prototypes from `Fighter*`/motion-state logic to `Item*`/`ItemKind` logic, updated symbol names for jump tables, annotated fighter-specific motion-state enum values, and adjusted mp/fighter struct types needed by the new matches.
what_changed: Implemented many previously stubbed `ftCo_800B*` routines in `ftcpuattack.c`, including `ftCo_800B630C`, `ftCo_800B63D8`, `ftCo_800B658C`, `ftCo_800B683C`, `ftCo_800B7180`, `ftCo_800B885C`, `ftCo_800B89CC`, `ftCo_800B9020`, `ftCo_800B920C`, `ftCo_800B9340`, `ftCo_800B9504`, `ftCo_800B9704`, `ftCo_800B9790`, `ftCo_800B98C8`, `ftCo_800B9A04`, `ftCo_800B9CBC`, `ftCo_800B9F90`, `ftCo_800BA080`, `ftCo_800BA160`, `ftCo_800BA224`, `ftCo_800BA2E8`, `ftCo_800BA674`, `ftCo_800BA9A0`, `ftCo_800BB104`, and `ftCo_800BB768`.

Added CPU command sequencing in `ftcpuattack.c` using helpers such as `ftCo_800B463C`, `ftCo_800B46B8`, and `ftCo_800B4880` with commands like `CpuCmd_WaitFor`, `CpuCmd_SetLstickX`, `CpuCmd_SetLstickY`, `CpuCmd_LstickXForward`, `CpuCmd_PressA`, `CpuCmd_ReleaseA`, `CpuCmd_PressR`, `CpuCmd_ReleaseR`, and `CpuCmd_Done`.

Added fighter-kind-specific CPU attack handling for visible cases in the diff, including Ness special-N/special-hi ranges, Yoshi special-air-S ranges, Samus charge-shot hold, Donkey special-N loop, Zelda special-S ranges, and special common-case handling for Donkey, Kirby, and Peach motion-state thresholds.
Changed `ftCo_800A5908`, `ftCo_800A59E4`, `ftCo_800A5A28`, and `ftCo_800A5A5C` to take `Item*` and inspect `ip->kind`, replacing incorrect or placeholder `Fighter*` motion-id checks.
Removed a local `inlineI2(Item_GObj*)` helper in `ftCo_0A01.c` and reused `ftCo_800A5908(GET_ITEM(fp->item_gobj))` at multiple callsites.
Updated `ftCo_800A4E8C` declaration from `UNK_RET ftCo_800A4E8C(UNK_PARAMS)` to `Fighter* ftCo_800A4E8C(Fighter*, Vec3*)`.

Changed `mpIsland_8005ACE8` callsites/prototypes so an explicit cast between `mp_UnkStruct0` and `mp_UnkStruct5` was no longer needed; the diff removes a TODO asking whether those types were the same.
Expanded `Fighter_804D64FC_t` in `fighter.h` with padding and newly exposed fields `xC` and `x20` after `cmdscripts`.

Renamed three data symbols in `config/GALE01/symbols.txt` from `ftCo_803C6118`, `ftCo_803C613C`, and `ftCo_803C61BC` to `jtbl_803C6118`, `jtbl_803C613C`, and `jtbl_803C61BC`, indicating they are jump tables.
Annotated several fighter motion-state enum entries with explicit hexadecimal state numbers in Donkey, Ness, Samus, Yoshi, and Zelda forward headers.
smart_moves: The item helper correction is a high-value matching/typing fix: replacing `Fighter*` motion-state tests with `Item*` kind tests made the helpers semantically consistent with their callsites and allowed repeated inline item-kind logic to collapse into a shared function.

The PR added just enough fighter-specific forward headers and enum value annotations to express hard-coded CPU AI ranges symbolically, rather than leaving all special-move checks as raw numeric motion IDs.
Renaming `ftCo_*` data symbols to `jtbl_*` for 0x803C6118, 0x803C613C, and 0x803C61BC records a useful identification of compiler jump tables instead of treating them as generic ftCommon data.

The mp type cleanup removed a suspicious cast and associated TODO, which suggests the match depended on choosing the correct island/platform struct type rather than forcing the compiler through a cast.

The implementation preserves address-based unknown names such as `ftCo_800B683C` while adding comments where behavior is clear, keeping the code searchable and stable without over-naming uncertain symbols.

The CPU delay routine `ftCo_800B63D8` expresses level-dependent randomness directly with `HSD_Randf()` and `CpuCmd_WaitFor`, making the behavior readable while retaining match-oriented integer casts and constants.
decomp_lessons: When a helper appears to test motion states but all practical callsites pass items through `GET_ITEM`, re-evaluate the parameter type; matching may require changing both the prototype and semantic field access from `motion_id` to `kind`.

Repeated small inline predicates can be a clue that an existing non-inlined helper has the right shape; replacing local inline logic with a shared typed helper can improve both match quality and maintainability.

For CPU AI code, fighter-specific motion-state ranges often need character forward headers and explicit enum offsets so comparisons like Ness special-N start/end or Zelda special-S loop/end can be represented symbolically.
Jump table data should be named with `jtbl_` once identified; this prevents future workers from mistaking table blobs for ordinary fighter data.
Matching large AI/state-machine functions may require coordinated header work across fighter, item, and map/platform systems, not just filling in the C file with logic.
Removing casts around map/platform helper calls is preferable when the underlying forward type can be corrected; casts may mask a wrong struct typedef that will block other matches.
naming_conventions: `jtbl_803Cxxxx` was used for identified jump-table symbols, replacing previous generic `ftCo_803Cxxxx` names.
Unknown or not-yet-named functions retained address-style names such as `ftCo_800B630C`, `ftCo_800B683C`, and `ftCo_800BA9A0`.
Typed local variables generally use decomp temporary names such as `temp_r31`, `temp_r0`, `var_r30`, `var_f1`, and stack names such as `sp78`, consistent with match-oriented decomp style.
Item helper parameters were normalized to `Item* ip`; fighter parameters remain `Fighter* fp`.
Motion-state enum comments use explicit hexadecimal values in comments such as `/* 15C */ ftNs_MS_SpecialNStart` and `/* 158 */ ftSs_MS_SpecialNHold`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Used exact `CpuCmd_*` command streams with one-frame waits and stick neutralization/release sequences to match CPU controller-script generation.
Represented signed stick constants directly, including values like `0x7F`, `0x81`, `0x50`, `0xB0`, and `-0x7F`, matching the byte-oriented controller command style.
Kept explicit casts in level delay calculations, for example `(int) (40 * temp_f1) + 5`, which is important for matching float-to-int ordering.
Used fighter-kind and motion-id range checks to structure dispatch instead of large opaque numeric conditionals where enum symbols were available.
Inserted padding and unknown fields into `Fighter_804D64FC_t` to expose offsets used by the matched code without over-defining the full struct.
Converted local inline item-kind filtering to calls through `ftCo_800A5908(GET_ITEM(fp->item_gobj))`, reducing duplicate control flow and aligning callsite codegen.
review_feedback: No human review comments were present in the provided slice.
The only issue comment was the decomp.dev bot report: GALE01 matched code increased to 42.82%, +0.40%, +15536 bytes, with 28 new `main/melee/ft/ftcpuattack` matches.
The bot listed the largest new matches as `ftCo_800B683C` (+2372 bytes), `ftCo_800BA9A0` (+1892), `ftCo_800BA2E8` (+908), `ftCo_800BA674` (+812), and `ftCo_800B732C` (+780).
follow_up_queries: Look for later PRs that name or further type `Fighter_x1A88_t` fields used by `ftcpuattack.c`.
Search for follow-up work around `ftCo_800B732C`, which the bot reported as a new match but was not visible in the provided function-name grep excerpt.
Check whether `ftCo_800B92D4` was already matched before this PR or added in another slice, since `ftCo_800B658C` calls it but the bot's new-match list did not include it.
Search for later symbol renames of the newly matched `ftCo_800B*` CPU attack routines once behavior became better understood.
Review future mp/island type changes involving `mp_UnkStruct0`, `mp_UnkStruct5`, `mpIsland_8005AC14`, and `mpIsland_8005ACE8`.
